text.title: Flyweight, Pattern Selection Matrix, and SOLID Mapping
text.summary: Flyweight shares intrinsic state across a large number of similar objects. Pattern selection matrix maps scenarios to the correct structural pattern. SOLID principle mapping shows which SOLID principle each pattern enforces.

rule.statement: Use Flyweight only when the application creates thousands (or millions) of similar objects and memory profiling shows that object count is a measurable bottleneck. Flyweight adds extrinsic state management complexity — only justified by demonstrated memory savings.
rule.rationale: Flyweight trades memory for complexity: the caller must now provide extrinsic state on every operation instead of storing it on the object. Without measured memory pressure, this complexity has no benefit.

list.item: Intrinsic state: shared, immutable state stored in the flyweight (character glyph, particle texture)
Extrinsic state: unique per instance, provided by the caller on each use (position, color, size)
FlyweightFactory: returns shared flyweight instances; uses a cache keyed by intrinsic state
Use cases: text rendering (thousands of character glyphs), game particle systems, geographic tile rendering
Modern alternative: numpy/pandas vectorized arrays or GPU buffers often solve the same problem without the pattern overhead
"Integrate legacy/third-party with incompatible interface" → Adapter (retrofit, post-design)
"Add cross-cutting behavior without subclassing: logging, caching, retry" → Decorator (stacks at runtime)
"Simplify complex subsystem for callers" → Facade (one entry point per common workflow)
"Control access: lazy loading, auth, caching, audit" → Proxy (select by type)
"Tree/hierarchy, treat leaf and branch uniformly" → Composite (same interface for all nodes)
"Two independent dimensions of variation, both must grow" → Bridge (design-time, prevents class explosion)
"Thousands of similar objects, memory profiling shows pressure" → Flyweight (intrinsic/extrinsic split)

antipattern.description: Applying Adapter when Facade is needed (or vice versa)
antipattern.reason: Adapter converts a single incompatible interface; Facade simplifies a multi-class subsystem. Using Adapter for a complex subsystem creates a thin wrapper that still exposes subsystem complexity. Using Facade for an interface mismatch obscures the actual conversion logic.

list.item: Open/Closed Principle (OCP): Decorator, Composite, Proxy — add behavior without modifying existing classes
Single Responsibility Principle (SRP): Facade — moves subsystem orchestration out of client code into one place
Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP): all seven — client code depends on abstractions (interfaces), not concrete classes
Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP): Adapter, Decorator, Proxy — substitutable for the wrapped object's interface
Composition over Inheritance: Decorator, Bridge, Composite — avoid subclassing hierarchies in favor of object composition
